splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether actuarial assumptions used to calculate employer withdrawal liability under ERISA must be locked in by the end of the prior plan year or may be selected afterward. This affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers participating in multiemployer pension plans.

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that the plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, as the statute does not require all assumptions to be fixed by year-end.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions must be determined by the end of the prior plan year, providing employers with greater predictability about potential withdrawal liability.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits split on whether federal courts may apply a judicially-created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending court-ordered conditions beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. This affects thousands of individuals who abscond from supervised release each year.

positions.label: Tolling applies
positions.summary: Holds that the supervised release term is tolled while a supervisee is a fugitive, so the clock stops running during absconding and the court retains jurisdiction to revoke.

positions.label: Tolling does not apply
positions.summary: Holds that there is no statutory authority for tolling supervised release terms and courts may not create such a doctrine, so the supervised release term continues to run even while the supervisee absconds.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: Circuits are split 6-to-3 on whether federal prisoners face the categorical bar of § 2244(b)(1) — which prohibits relitigating any previously presented claim — in addition to § 2255(h)'s requirements for successive motions. The outcome determines whether federal prisoners face a stricter gatekeeping requirement than Congress may have intended.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1)'s bar on previously raised claims applies to federal prisoners' successive § 2255 motions, categorically barring any claim previously presented.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions, meaning federal prisoners need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s requirements of newly discovered evidence or a new rule of constitutional law.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether candidates have standing to bring pre-election challenges to ballot-counting rules. The outcome determines whether such disputes must wait until after the election, with significant implications for voting procedures nationwide.

positions.label: Candidate has standing
positions.summary: Holds that a candidate suffers a cognizable competitive injury from election rules that allegedly dilute lawful votes, sufficient for Article III standing to challenge those rules pre-election.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

positions.label: Candidate lacks standing
positions.summary: Holds that a candidate's alleged competitive injury from ballot-counting procedures is too speculative or generalized to confer Article III standing before an election has occurred.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity takes precedence over agency or Sentencing Commission deference (Auer/Kisor deference) when interpreting ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether lenity trumps deference to agency interpretations in the criminal context. At least three circuits hold lenity prevails over deference, while the Third Circuit has held that agency deference trumps lenity. The issue has grown more significant after Loper Bright overruled Chevron.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity takes precedence over Auer/Kisor deference when interpreting ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions, preventing agencies from expanding criminal liability through interpretive guidance.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that agency or Sentencing Commission deference takes precedence over the rule of lenity in interpreting ambiguous Guidelines enhancements, deferring to the anti-defendant reading.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify a defendant) or disjunctive (any one criterion is disqualifying).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply split on whether 'and' in the safety-valve provision means a defendant must meet all three criminal-history criteria to be disqualified from safety-valve relief, or whether meeting any one criterion is sufficient. This directly affects mandatory minimum sentencing for drug offenses across the country.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), reading 'and' in its ordinary conjunctive sense.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria is sufficient to disqualify a defendant from safety-valve relief, reading 'and' as 'or' based on statutory context and structure.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2, which includes inchoate offenses like conspiracy in the definition of 'controlled substance offense,' is entitled to deference when the guideline text itself does not mention inchoate offenses.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the Sentencing Commission's commentary expanding the career-offender guideline's definition of 'controlled substance offense' to include conspiracy and other inchoate offenses is a valid, deferrable interpretation or impermissibly expands the guideline's text. This affects the applicability of significant sentencing enhancements.

positions.label: Commentary is entitled to deference
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1 is the Commission's authoritative interpretation of § 4B1.2(b) and is entitled to controlling deference under Stinson, meaning conspiracy qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' for career-offender purposes.

positions.label: Commentary is not entitled to deference
positions.summary: Holds that the guideline text unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses, so Application Note 1 impermissibly expands the definition and is not entitled to deference under Kisor's requirement to exhaust traditional tools of construction first.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the state schedule includes substances not listed on the federal controlled substances schedules.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the career-offender guideline's definition of 'controlled substance offense' requires the substance underlying a state conviction to also be federally controlled. The Eighth Circuit holds no such federal-law limitation exists, while other circuits disagree. The Supreme Court addressed a related issue in Brown v. United States (2024).

positions.label: No federal-law limitation required
positions.summary: Holds that § 4B1.2(b) broadly defines 'controlled substance offense' to include state offenses regardless of whether the substance is also controlled under federal law, finding no textual basis to graft a federal limitation.

positions.label: Federal-law limitation applies
positions.summary: Holds that a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' only if the substance involved is also controlled under federal law, applying the categorical approach.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a non-retroactive change in sentencing law, such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c), can constitute or contribute to an 'extraordinary and compelling reason' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits are divided on whether non-retroactive changes in sentencing law may be considered as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release. Some circuits flatly prohibit it, while others allow it. The Sentencing Commission's 2024 amendment to U.S.S.G. § 1B1.13(b)(6) attempted to allow it, but some circuits have held the Commission exceeded its authority.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes in law may be considered
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in sentencing law may be considered, alone or in combination with other factors, as extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes in law may not be considered
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in law cannot contribute to extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release, and that the Sentencing Commission's policy statement attempting to allow it exceeds its statutory authority.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of a debtor's asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to the debtor upon good-faith conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether appreciation in property value between the filing of a Chapter 13 petition and conversion to Chapter 7 belongs to the bankruptcy estate or the debtor. The Ninth Circuit held it belongs to the estate, while other circuits have reached the opposite conclusion.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that the plain language of § 348(f)(1)(A), coupled with the broad scope of § 541(a), means post-petition appreciation inures to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion, not to the debtor.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that § 348(f)(1)(A) was designed to protect good-faith converting debtors, and post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation in property value belongs to the debtor, not the estate.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may expressly impose a sentence upon revocation of supervised release to punish a defendant for violating the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether punishment is a permissible purpose of a revocation sentence. The Sixth Circuit allows district courts expressly to punish defendants for violations of supervised release, while other circuits hold that the statutory purpose of revocation sentences is limited to addressing the breach of trust, not punishment.

positions.label: Punishment is permissible
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may expressly punish defendants for violations of supervised release when imposing a revocation sentence.

positions.label: Punishment is not a permissible purpose
positions.summary: Holds that supervised release revocation sentences should address the defendant's breach of trust and the need to protect the public, not serve as punishment for the violation conduct itself.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the appeal deadline for a Hyde Amendment attorney's fees order is the 30-day civil deadline or the 14-day criminal deadline under the Federal Rules of Appellate Procedure.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case (triggering the 30-day civil appeal deadline) or part of the criminal case itself (triggering the shorter 14-day criminal deadline). The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies.

positions.label: Civil deadline (30 days) applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, so the 30-day civil appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a) applies.

positions.label: Criminal deadline (14 days) applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion arises from and is part of the criminal case, so the shorter criminal appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(b) applies.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether federal prisoners must bring challenges to conditions of confinement — including BOP decisions affecting the duration of custody such as First Step Act time credits — through habeas corpus or may use civil rights actions (e.g., Bivens or the APA).
splits.description: A longstanding circuit split exists on whether habeas corpus is the exclusive federal remedy for prisoner challenges that implicate the fact or duration of confinement, or whether civil rights or APA actions may also be used. This affects how federal prisoners challenge BOP decisions on earned time credits, prerelease custody, and similar matters under the First Step Act.

positions.label: Habeas is the exclusive remedy for duration-of-confinement claims
positions.summary: Holds that when a prisoner's challenge goes to the fact or duration of confinement, habeas corpus is the sole federal remedy, and civil rights or APA claims are not permissible alternatives.

positions.label: Both habeas and civil rights actions may be available
positions.summary: Holds that conditions-of-confinement challenges and certain BOP decision challenges may be cognizable under civil rights statutes or the APA, not only habeas, even when they affect the duration of custody.

circuits.name: Third Circuit
circuits.caseName: Woodall v. Federal Bureau of Prisons

splits.legalQuestion: Whether an order denying a motion to seal medical records in litigation is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine.
splits.description: The D.C. Circuit held that denial of a motion to seal documents referencing medical treatments and diagnoses is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine because medical privacy is a 'value of high order.' Other circuits have taken varying approaches to immediate appealability of sealing orders, creating potential disagreement on this issue.

positions.label: Immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that denial of a motion to seal medical records meets the collateral order doctrine's requirements because maintaining privacy in medical treatments and diagnoses is a value of 'high order' warranting immediate appellate review.

positions.label: Not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that orders denying motions to seal, including those involving medical information, are not immediately appealable collateral orders because the privacy interest can be adequately vindicated on appeal from final judgment.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the FAAAA's broad preemption clause covers state negligence claims against freight brokers for injuries arising from carrier accidents. Some circuits find preemption while others do not, with significant implications for the trucking and logistics industry.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that the FAAAA's preemption clause bars state-law negligence claims against freight brokers because such claims relate to the broker's 'services' in arranging transportation.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Miller v. C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are not preempted by the FAAAA because they are generally applicable safety standards, not regulations of broker services.

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether claims that overlap with habeas corpus relief — such as alleged legal errors in the underlying conviction — may be considered as extraordinary and compelling reasons for a sentence reduction under the compassionate release statute. Some circuits permit consideration while others categorically bar it.

positions.label: May consider conviction-related errors
positions.summary: Holds that district courts have discretion to consider a broad range of factors, including potential legal errors in the conviction or sentence, as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

positions.label: May not consider conviction-related errors
positions.summary: Holds that § 3582(c)(1)(A) is not a vehicle for challenging the validity of a conviction or sentence, and such claims must be raised through habeas corpus under § 2255.

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of discrimination or unfair treatment in the operation of hospitality or business establishments under 42 U.S.C. § 1981 or related civil rights statutes?
splits.description: The Supreme Court granted certiorari to review the Second Circuit's approach to discrimination claims in hospitality settings, indicating a circuit split on the applicable legal standards for such claims. The case will clarify standards for fair treatment, discrimination liability, and damages in service industries nationwide.

positions.label: Second Circuit approach
positions.summary: Applied a particular legal framework to discrimination claims arising from hospitality establishments that the Supreme Court found sufficiently unsettled to warrant review.

positions.label: Differing approach by other circuits
positions.summary: Other circuits have applied different standards to discrimination claims in commercial or hospitality contexts, creating the split that prompted SCOTUS review.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3), which prohibits unlawful users of controlled substances from possessing firearms, violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.
splits.description: Following the Supreme Court's decision in New York State Rifle & Pistol Ass'n v. Bruen, circuits have split on whether § 922(g)(3)'s prohibition on firearm possession by users of controlled substances, particularly marijuana, is consistent with the Second Amendment's text and the nation's historical tradition of firearms regulation.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is constitutional
positions.summary: Holds that the historical tradition of disarming those who pose a danger to themselves or others, including habitual substance users, supports the constitutionality of § 922(g)(3) even after Bruen.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is unconstitutional as applied
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because the government cannot demonstrate a sufficient historical analogue for disarming non-violent drug users.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether workers who distribute baked goods to retail stores qualify as 'transportation workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, thereby exempting them from mandatory arbitration agreements.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on the scope of the Section 1 exemption for transportation workers, particularly whether last-mile delivery workers who distribute goods to retail stores are 'engaged in . . . interstate commerce.' This affects the enforceability of arbitration agreements for a wide range of distribution workers.

positions.label: Broad exemption — last-mile delivery workers qualify
positions.summary: Holds that workers who are part of a continuous interstate transportation chain, including those making last-mile deliveries of goods that have moved in interstate commerce, qualify as transportation workers exempt from the FAA.

positions.label: Narrow exemption — last-mile distributors do not qualify
positions.summary: Holds that distribution workers delivering goods locally to retail stores are not 'transportation workers engaged in . . . interstate commerce' under Section 1 because they are not themselves moving goods across state lines.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims based on allegedly inadequate product labeling when the EPA has not required the specific warning.
splits.description: The circuits disagree on whether FIFRA's preemption provision bars state-law failure-to-warn claims against pesticide manufacturers when the claims are based on labeling that the EPA approved. This affects product liability litigation involving agricultural chemicals and consumer pesticides nationwide.

positions.label: FIFRA preempts failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims that would require labeling different from or in addition to what the EPA has approved.

positions.label: FIFRA does not preempt failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA does not preempt state-law failure-to-warn claims because they impose requirements 'parallel to' rather than 'in addition to' FIFRA's labeling requirements, consistent with the Supreme Court's Bates v. Dow Agrosciences framework.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Monsanto Company v. John L. Durnell

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Religious Land Use and Institutionalized Persons Act (RLUIPA) authorizes individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether RLUIPA creates a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials in their individual capacities. Some circuits have held that RLUIPA, enacted under the Spending Clause, does not clearly authorize personal-liability damages against individuals who are not direct recipients of federal funds, while others have allowed such claims to proceed.

positions.label: RLUIPA authorizes individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA provides a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials sued in their individual capacities for violations of prisoners' religious exercise rights.

positions.label: RLUIPA does not authorize individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA does not clearly authorize individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials because Congress cannot impose personal liability on non-recipient individuals through the Spending Clause.

circuits.name: Fifth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Sossamon v. Lone Star State of Texas

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Landor v. Louisiana Department of Corrections and Public Safety

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 47(b) of the Investment Company Act creates a private right of action allowing parties to a contract that violates the ICA to sue in federal court for rescission of that contract.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether § 47(b) of the ICA provides an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts made in violation of the Act. This affects the ability of private parties to enforce the ICA's protections against abusive investment company practices through direct federal court actions.

positions.label: Section 47(b) creates a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) creates an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts that violate the Investment Company Act, allowing private parties to sue directly.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

positions.label: Section 47(b) does not create a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) is a voidability provision rather than a private right of action, and only the SEC can enforce the ICA's prohibitions.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, as construed under the Gingles framework, can constitutionally compel race-based redistricting as a remedy for vote dilution, and whether compliance with Section 2 constitutes a compelling governmental interest justifying the use of race in drawing district lines.
splits.description: This case raises the question of whether race-conscious redistricting mandated by Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act is constitutionally permissible under the Equal Protection Clause. Lower courts have applied the Gingles framework to require consideration of race, but the constitutionality of this framework as applied is increasingly contested.

positions.label: Section 2 compels constitutionally permissible race-conscious redistricting
positions.summary: Holds that compliance with Section 2 of the VRA, as interpreted under the Gingles framework, constitutes a compelling interest that justifies the use of race in drawing district lines.

positions.label: Section 2's compelled race-based redistricting raises constitutional concerns
positions.summary: Holds or suggests that the Gingles framework's requirement of race-based redistricting may exceed constitutional limits under the Equal Protection Clause, particularly after Dobbs-era equal protection jurisprudence.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Arkansas State Conference of the NAACP v. Arkansas Board of Apportionment
